era_context: The early 16th century: the Renaissance flourishing in Italy and spreading throughout Europe; the Ottoman Empire expanding under Selim I; the Ming Dynasty ruling China; the Aztec Empire at its height in Mesoamerica; European maritime exploration accelerating; the Catholic Church dominant in Western Europe but facing growing criticism of corruption and ecclesiastical abuses.

events.title: Martin Luther publishes the Ninety-Five Theses; beginning of the Protestant Reformation
events.description: Martin Luther, an Augustinian friar and theology professor at the University of Wittenberg, publishes or posts his Ninety-Five Theses on October 31, 1517 (the date is traditional; some scholars debate whether he formally posted them on the church door or simply circulated them). The Theses critique the Catholic Church's practice of selling indulgences (remissions of penance for sins) and challenge papal authority. Luther's action, intended as an academic disputation, becomes a catalyst for the Protestant Reformation. The printing press rapidly disseminates Luther's ideas throughout Europe, sparking widespread debate about ecclesiastical corruption, salvation, and the authority of the Church. This event marks a fundamental turning point in Western Christianity and European history, leading to the fragmentation of Western Christendom and profound social, political, and cultural consequences.
key_figures: Johann Tetzel (indulgence seller, criticized by Luther)

sources.name: Luther, Martin. Ninety-Five Theses (Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum), 1517
Luther, Martin. Various letters and writings, 1517
Brecht, Martin. Martin Luther: His Road to Reformation, 1985

events.certainty_note: The publication or circulation of Luther's Ninety-Five Theses in October 1517 is confirmed by multiple contemporary sources and modern scholarship. The exact date of October 31 is traditional but not definitively documented in contemporary sources.

events.title: Selim I conquers the Mamluk Sultanate; Ottoman Empire expands to Egypt and the Levant
events.description: Selim I, the Ottoman Sultan, defeats the Mamluk Sultanate in a series of military campaigns culminating in the Battle of Ridaniyya (or Marj al-Dabiq) in August 1516 and the final conquest of Cairo in January 1517. These victories give the Ottoman Empire control over Egypt, Syria, the Hijaz (including Mecca and Medina), and the eastern Mediterranean. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate represents a major shift in Middle Eastern geopolitics, as the Ottoman Empire becomes the dominant Islamic power and the guardian of Islam's holiest sites. The incorporation of Egypt and the Levant into the Ottoman Empire significantly increases Ottoman wealth, power, and prestige. The conquest also marks the end of the Mamluk Sultanate, which had ruled Egypt and the Levant for nearly 300 years.
key_figures: Tuman Bay II (last Mamluk Sultan, executed 1517)
events.certainty_note: The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate by Selim I is well-documented in both Ottoman and Mamluk sources. The Battle of Ridaniyya occurred in August 1516, and the final conquest of Cairo was completed in January 1517. The date of 1517 marks the consolidation of Ottoman control over Egypt and the Levant.

events.title: Portuguese establish Fort São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle) in West Africa
events.description: The Portuguese, under the direction of Prince Henry the Navigator and subsequent Portuguese monarchs, establish and consolidate control over Fort São Jorge da Mina (Elmina Castle) on the coast of present-day Ghana. The fort, originally constructed in 1482, becomes the center of Portuguese trading activities in West Africa. By 1517, the Portuguese have established a network of trading posts along the West African coast, facilitating the trade in gold, ivory, and enslaved people. The Portuguese use military force and naval superiority to establish monopolies over West African trade. The establishment of Portuguese trading posts in West Africa marks the beginning of European colonial presence in Africa and the intensification of the Atlantic slave trade, which will have profound demographic, economic, and social consequences for Africa and the Americas.

sources.name: Portuguese colonial records and trading company documents
Eltis, David, and David Richardson. Atlas of the Transatlantic Slave Trade, 2010

events.certainty_note: The Portuguese presence at Elmina Castle and other West African trading posts is well-documented in Portuguese colonial records. The fort was established in 1482 and consolidated in the early 16th century, including 1517.

events.title: Montezuma II rules the Aztec Empire at its height
events.description: Montezuma II (also known as Moctezuma II), the ninth emperor of the Aztec Empire, continues to rule the empire at the height of its territorial extent and power. Montezuma II ascended to the throne in 1502 and ruled until his death in 1520. During his reign, the Aztec Empire expanded its territorial control and tributary system, extracting tribute from a vast network of subject peoples. The empire is characterized by sophisticated urban centers, advanced agricultural systems, complex religious practices, and a hierarchical social structure. However, the empire also faces internal tensions and resistance from tributary states. In 1517, the Aztec Empire is at the apex of its power, though it will soon face the challenge of Spanish conquest initiated by Cortés's landing in 1519.

sources.name: Sahagún, Bernardino de. Florentine Codex (Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España), compiled 1558–1582
Durán, Diego. The History of the Indies of New Spain (Historia de las Indias de Nueva España), written c. 1581

events.certainty_note: Montezuma II's rule of the Aztec Empire in 1517 is confirmed by indigenous sources and Spanish colonial records. His reign (1502–1520) is well-documented, though specific events in individual years are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty continues rule of China; Zheng He's voyages ongoing
events.description: The Ming Dynasty continues to rule China in 1517 under the Jiajing Emperor (r. 1521–1567, but the succession was being prepared in 1517). The Ming Dynasty, which began in 1368, is characterized by centralized imperial rule, the construction of the Great Wall, the Forbidden City, and other monumental architecture, and the sponsorship of maritime exploration under Admiral Zheng He. In 1517, the Ming court continues to support Zheng He's treasure voyages, which represent the height of Chinese maritime power and exploration. The Ming Dynasty is also characterized by sophisticated bureaucratic systems, Confucian scholarship, and cultural achievements in art, literature, and philosophy.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty), official court records

events.certainty_note: The Ming Dynasty's rule of China in 1517 is confirmed by official court records and modern scholarship. The Zhengde Emperor was ruling in 1517, and Zheng He's voyages were ongoing during this period.

events.title: Reformation ideas spread throughout Europe; printing press accelerates dissemination
events.description: Following Martin Luther's publication of the Ninety-Five Theses in October 1517, reformist ideas spread rapidly throughout Europe, facilitated by the printing press. Luther's writings are translated into various European languages and circulated widely among clergy, intellectuals, and common people. The rapid dissemination of Luther's ideas creates a broader movement for religious reform that extends beyond Germany. Universities, monasteries, and printing centers throughout Europe engage with Luther's critique of the Church and indulgences. The printing press, invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the mid-15th century, proves to be a revolutionary technology for the spread of ideas, enabling the rapid dissemination of Luther's writings and the Protestant Reformation.

sources.name: Luther, Martin. Various writings and letters, 1517
Eisenstein, Elizabeth L. The Printing Press as an Agent of Change, 1979

events.certainty_note: The rapid spread of Luther's ideas throughout Europe in 1517 and the following years is confirmed by contemporary sources and modern scholarship. The role of the printing press in disseminating reformist ideas is well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of Luther's posting of the Ninety-Five Theses on October 31, 1517 is debated by modern scholars. Some argue that Luther may not have formally posted them on the church door but rather circulated them for academic discussion. The significance of the Theses as a catalyst for the Reformation is confirmed, but the traditional narrative of a dramatic public posting may be somewhat exaggerated. The conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate by Selim I is dated to 1516–1517, with the final conquest of Cairo in January 1517, but some sources place the decisive battle (Ridaniyya) in August 1516. The Portuguese presence in West Africa and the slave trade were already well-established before 1517, so the date represents consolidation rather than initiation.
historiographic_note: The year 1517 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly regarding the Protestant Reformation. However, the historical record is heavily shaped by European perspectives and the interests of Protestant and Catholic historians. Ottoman and Mamluk sources provide information about Selim I's conquest, though these are often filtered through imperial perspectives. Portuguese colonial records document trading activities in West Africa and Asia, but these sources reflect Portuguese interests and biases. Indigenous American sources are sparse for this year, as most were recorded after the Spanish conquest. Chinese sources regarding the Ming Dynasty and Zheng He's voyages are official court records, which may omit or downplay certain activities. Overall, 1517 is a year of global significance, but the historical record is unevenly distributed across regions, with Europe and the Ottoman Empire well-documented and other regions less thoroughly represented.

graph_edges.note: Luther's Ninety-Five Theses in 1517 led to wider circulation and debate of his ideas in 1518
Luther's Theses in 1517 eventually led to his excommunication in 1520
Selim I's conquest of the Mamluk Sultanate in 1517 led to consolidation of Ottoman power in 1518
Portuguese trading activities in West Africa in 1517 were contemporary with expansion in Asia in 1518
The spread of reformist ideas in Europe in 1517 led to Zwingli's public reformist preaching in Switzerland in 1519
